ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for breaded fried chicken strips; mapped to broader chicken food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKN covers cooked chicken meat; breaded fried preparation is not separately coded. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for breaded fried chicken strips as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chicken strips in Singapore consists of approximately 5 to 6 pieces weighing around 150 g, commonly served as a main dish or snack with dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Strips — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Strips (cr_id G00010448). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for breaded fried chicken (NDB 05062) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean-style breaded fried chicken strips. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous breaded fried chicken products from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care). The breaded coating (refined wheat flour and breadcrumbs) is the primary glycaemic driver. GL per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (22.0 g/100g × 150 g serving = 33.0 g) / 100 = 21.45, rounded to 21.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 798 mg per 100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving delivers approximately 1197 mg sodium, representing nearly the full daily DASH diet sodium limit of 1500 mg; individuals with hypertension should avoid this food or strictly limit portion size and frequency.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate coating (22 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g) and estimated medium GI of 65 promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The breaded coating contributes rapidly digestible refined carbohydrates with a glycaemic load of approximately 21.5 per 150 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption and prefer grilled or baked unbreaded chicken to minimise postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (798 mg/100 g), saturated fat (estimated 3.2 g/100 g), and trans fat from deep frying (estimated 0.2 g/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium, saturated fat, and trans fat from deep-frying makes this food unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; choose baked or grilled chicken preparations with minimal added salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; high sodium worsens RA-related oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried AGEs are a primary pro-inflammatory trigger in rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA should avoid this food and choose baked or grilled chicken with low-sodium seasoning.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Strips are elongated pieces of chicken breast or tenderloin coated in seasoned breadcrumbs or batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. In Singapore, they are commonly served as a snack, fast-food item, or side dish, often accompanied by chilli sauce, honey mustard, or sweet-and-sour dipping sauces. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned, while the interior remains tender and juicy. Per 100 g, chicken strips provide 295 kcal, 19.2 g protein, 14.5 g fat, and 22.0 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 798 mg driven by seasoning and processing. The refined breadcrumb coating contributes rapidly digestible carbohydrates with low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g), resulting in an estimated medium glycaemic index of 65 and a glycaemic load of approximately 21.5 per 150 g serving. Clinically, the high sodium content makes this food unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, while the refined carbohydrate coating and medium-high glycaemic load are concerns for Type 2 Diabetes management. The deep-frying process generates trans fats and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), raising cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. Chicken strips are not suitable for regular consumption by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Healthier alternatives include grilled or baked unbreaded chicken with reduced-sodium seasoning.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken strips?
Is chicken strips high in sodium?
Are chicken fingers good for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010448, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for breaded fried chicken (NDB 05062) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this item. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for breaded fried chicken products.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (5.8 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (3.9 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for breaded fried chicken (NDB 05062) as the HPB source does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this item. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA for this specific preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed vitamin, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean chicken strips preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status assumed based on typical Singaporean food service preparation; individual vendor verification recommended. 5. Trans fat estimated at 0.2 g/100 g from deep-frying; this is consistent with the input health_suitability notes. 6. FoodEx2 match type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'adapted') to conform to the valid enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] required by the scoring model.
